nativeRange.habitat: Ephemeral seasonal pools, temporary swamps, ditches, and small streams on Zanzibar's Unguja Island. Pools dry completely each dry season, requiring eggs to survive months without water.

decorPreferences: peat substrate or peat moss spawning layer

diet.notes: Micro-carnivore that tends to do best on live and frozen foods. Brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, microworms, and white worms are commonly recommended. Small portions fed twice daily are generally advised. Some individuals have been reported to accept dried foods with conditioning, but live prey is preferred for optimal coloring and breeding condition.

goodWith: small peaceful killifish in separate tanks
avoidWith: fast-swimming community fish that outcompete for food

summary: A vividly colored annual killifish endemic to Zanzibar's seasonal pools. Males display striking red-orange and blue iridescence; females are drab. Lifespan is generally 9–15 months; eggs survive months of dry diapause before hatching when rewetted.
careNotes:
ANNUAL SPECIES: Nothobranchius guentheri has a genetically-programmed lifespan typically ranging from around 9 months in the wild to 15–18 months in well-kept aquariums. This is not a sign of poor husbandry — the fish completes its full life cycle within roughly one year as an adaptation to ephemeral seasonal pools that dry out annually. Cooler water (closer to 18–20°C) is generally advised to slow the aging process slightly; warmer temperatures are reported to shorten lifespan further.

ESCAPE RISK: This species is a strong jumper. A tight-fitting, fully enclosed lid is considered essential — even small gaps around equipment can allow escape.

SEXUAL DIMORPHISM: Males display vivid red-orange and iridescent blue coloration and tend to reach 5–6 cm. Females are significantly smaller (approximately 3.5–4 cm) and brown, making them easy to misidentify as juveniles.

CONSPECIFIC AGGRESSION: Males have been known to be highly aggressive toward one another, with fin-tearing and occasionally fatal outcomes in confined space. A ratio of one male to two females is commonly advised; species-only setups minimize competition and stress.

BREEDING DIAPAUSE: Egg incubation at room temperature (20–24°C) in semi-dried peat typically requires approximately 6–12 weeks, with most eggs hatching around 10 weeks. This is considerably shorter than the sibling species Nothobranchius rachovii (5–9 months), making N. guentheri more accessible to newcomers to annual killifish keeping. Re-wet peat with aged soft water to trigger hatching.

DISEASE: This species has been documented as susceptible to velvet (Oodinium) disease. Some experienced keepers add a small amount of aquarium salt as a prophylactic measure; consult current husbandry guidance before doing so.

CONSERVATION: N. guentheri is listed as Endangered (EN) on the IUCN Red List (assessed 2018), endemic solely to Zanzibar's Unguja Island. Hobbyists are generally advised to maintain locality-pure strains and avoid hybridization with related species.

CROSS-REFERENCE: See also Bluefin Notho (Nothobranchius rachovii, fw-fish-062) — a closely related sibling species from Mozambique with a longer diapause period and similar husbandry requirements.
breedingNotes: Provide a peat moss substrate layer (approximately 1–2 cm deep) on the tank bottom as a spawning medium. Males court females vigorously; pairs push eggs into the peat during spawning. After spawning, remove and semi-dry the peat, seal in a bag or container at 20–24°C, and store for approximately 6–12 weeks (most eggs hatch around 10 weeks per British Killifish Association data). Re-wet the peat with aged soft water to trigger hatching. Fry are large enough to accept newly-hatched brine shrimp immediately and grow rapidly, reaching spawning maturity in approximately 4 weeks with good feeding. Eggs tolerate shipping dry, making egg exchange among hobbyists a common trade method.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed taxonomy (Cyprinodontiformes / Nothobranchiidae), native range (Unguja Island, Zanzibar, Tanzania), water parameters (22–25°C, pH 6.0–7.0, dH 4–10), spawning behavior (bottom spawner, 3–4 months field incubation note), and conservation status (EN). Max size reported as 3.5 cm SL male — consistent with ~5 cm TL once tail length is included.

additional.name: British Killifish Association
additional.notes: High-quality specialist source. Cross-checked adult size (males to 6.4 cm, females to 3.8 cm), temperature range (18–26°C), pH (6.2–7.0), lifespan (15–18 months captivity), diapause duration (6–12 weeks, typically ~10 weeks), conspecific aggression, diet preferences, and velvet disease susceptibility. The 2–3 week diapause figure from aqua-fish.net was treated as an outlier in favor of this source's 6–12 week range.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6–7 cm), temperature (22–25°C), pH (6.5–8.0), minimum tank (38 L), lifespan (9–14 months), diet, and compatible tankmates. Diapause figure of 2–3 weeks appears anomalously short compared to BKA and Wikipedia; not used.

fish.breedingNotes: Peat substrate spawning with egg diapause of approximately 6–12 weeks dry storage (shorter than most Nothobranchius; one factor making this species accessible to annual killifish beginners). Eggs are robust and shippable; the killifish hobbyist trade is conducted primarily via egg exchange rather than live fish sales.
